settings.title: Free Portraits for the Animal Shelter
settings.excerpt: A good photo can be the difference between an overlooked dog and one that finds a home.
settings.body:
We have spent a few Saturdays this winter at the county shelter with our lights and a lot of patience.

Shelter listings live or die on their photos. A dog shot in a dim kennel looks frightened, the same dog shot with a little care looks like family. The difference shows up in how fast they get adopted.

What a few minutes of light changes

Most kennel photos are taken on a phone through a wire door under hard overhead light, and even a calm dog looks tense in them. We bring a soft light, get down to their level, and wait for the moment the animal relaxes. That one frame, where the ears come up and the eyes settle, is what makes someone stop scrolling.

A morning at the shelter usually looks like this.

  • A quiet space set aside so the animals are not spooked.
  • Time on the floor with each one until they ease up.
  • A handful of edited shots sent straight to the listing staff.

We are donating one morning a month through the year to photograph animals waiting for homes. If you have ever met a pet through a screen first, you know exactly why this matters to us.

How you can help

The photos do the most good when they travel. If you see one of these listings, a share costs nothing and genuinely shortens the wait for a dog or cat sitting in a kennel.

If you run a local rescue with the same problem, get in touch. We cannot cover everyone, but we would rather spend a free morning on this than almost anything else we could photograph that day.
